coredata.dc:title: Data-driven modeling of lithium-ion battery degradation using XGBoost with extended Kalman filter-based internal resistance correction

Ashidqi, M. Dzaky
Nizam, Muhammad
Purwanto, Agus
coredata.dc:description:
Accurate prediction of battery degradation is essential for the reliability and cost-effectiveness of lithium-ion batteries in energy storage. Previous degradation prediction methods such as model-based methods provide interpretability but rely on simplifications, whereas data-driven approaches capture nonlinearities yet lack robustness to noise and extrapolation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a hybrid degradation modeling framework that integrates extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) with an extended Kalman filter (EKF) correction. Experimental data were collected from 2000 continuous charge–discharge cycles of lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells. Statistical features were extracted from current and voltage profiles to train the XGBoost model for capacity fade prediction. While the XGBoost model effectively learns complex degradation patterns, its predictions may be affected by operational uncertainties. To enhance robustness, the EKF was employed to estimate internal resistance in real time based on a Thevenin equivalent circuit model, and this estimation was used as a correction term for the XGBoost outputs. This hybrid design combines the accuracy of machine learning with the physical interpretability and stability of model-based estimation. Validation results show that the proposed method achieves a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.041, mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 3.47%, and coefficient of determination (
R
2
) of 0.98. In addition, the framework maintains low computational cost, making it suitable for real-time embedded applications. These results highlight the potential of the proposed method for reliable state-of-health monitoring and predictive maintenance of advanced battery energy storage systems.

Computationally efficient, easier implementation, suitable for real-time use

Good with small datasets (SVM), uncertainty quantification (GPR)
SVM struggles with nonlinearities; GPR has high computational cost
Excellent for nonlinear modeling; effective long-term prediction; works with image-based data
Needs large datasets; risk of overfitting; limited interpretability [34,35]
Robust to missing data; high accuracy with small datasets; fast training
Offline training limits adaptability; struggles with noisy or unseen conditions
Combines physical insights with ML flexibility; improved robustness and accuracy

head.ce:title: Data-driven modeling of lithium-ion battery degradation using XGBoost with extended Kalman filter-based internal resistance correction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Accurate prediction of battery degradation is essential for the reliability and cost-effectiveness of lithium-ion batteries in energy storage. Previous degradation prediction methods such as model-based methods provide interpretability but rely on simplifications, whereas data-driven approaches capture nonlinearities yet lack robustness to noise and extrapolation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a hybrid degradation modeling framework that integrates extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) with an extended Kalman filter (EKF) correction. Experimental data were collected from 2000 continuous charge–discharge cycles of lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells. Statistical features were extracted from current and voltage profiles to train the XGBoost model for capacity fade prediction. While the XGBoost model effectively learns complex degradation patterns, its predictions may be affected by operational uncertainties. To enhance robustness, the EKF was employed to estimate internal resistance in real time based on a Thevenin equivalent circuit model, and this estimation was used as a correction term for the XGBoost outputs. This hybrid design combines the accuracy of machine learning with the physical interpretability and stability of model-based estimation. Validation results show that the proposed method achieves a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.041, mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 3.47%, and coefficient of determination (
R
2
) of 0.98. In addition, the framework maintains low computational cost, making it suitable for real-time embedded applications. These results highlight the potential of the proposed method for reliable state-of-health monitoring and predictive maintenance of advanced battery energy storage systems.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive utilization of Lithium-ion battery in various applications has required improvement in reliability [1] [2] [3]. One of the most significant challenge in battery reliability is battery degradation. Battery degradation significantly impacts system performance, reliability, and economic viability [4] [5]. Therefore, the degradation of the lithium-ion battery should be modeled accurately to support the reliability and the performance of the system. However, understanding and predicting the degradation processes of batteries remains a critical challenge [6]. This challenge is compounded by the highly nonlinear battery degradation mechanisms including chemical, thermal, and mechanical effects [7] [8]. Therefore, developing robust and accurate models for battery degradation has become a significant gap to be solved [9].

Existing approaches for estimating capacity loss or state of health (SOH) can be categorized into three main types: physics-based methods, data-driven methods, and hybrid methods. Each approach has distinct advantages and limitations, which are illustrated in Table 1 . In addition to these estimation techniques, direct measurement approaches including constant current (CC) discharge tests, incremental capacity (IC) analysis, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), are commonly used in laboratory settings to obtain accurate SOH values [10] [11]. These methods provide high accuracy and diagnostic insight into degradation mechanisms. However, such methods are typically time-consuming, intrusive, and impractical for real-time or onboard battery management applications [12]. Therefore, model-based estimation methods remain the focus for online monitoring and control.

Data-driven methods offer faster computation and better adaptability to complex, nonlinear battery behavior, making them suitable for real-time applications [13]. These methods utilize algorithms that can learn battery degradation patterns directly from operational data without relying on explicit physical modeling, enabling flexible modeling of various battery chemistries and usage conditions. However, they often require large datasets, may overfit and lack physical interpretability, highlighting the continued need for physics-based models to ensure robustness, accuracy, and consistency with actual battery dynamics [14].

Physics-based models have been widely developed to learn battery degradation mechanisms. These models are generally chategorized into two types including electrochemical models [15] [16] and electrical equivalent circuit model (ECM) [17] [18]. However, these models are not able to handle high nonlinearity on battery degradation mechanism [19]. Physic-based methods rely on physical theory including electrical and electrochemical theorem. The model developed as representations of battery physical behavior. Electrochemical models (EM), such as Coupled electrochemical-thermal-degradation model [20], provide a detailed depiction of internal processes, including solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer growth, lithium plating, and loss of active materials. This model integrates a one-dimensional electrochemical model (1D-EM), a three-dimensional thermal model (3D-TM), and a degradation model to predict battery degradation under various temperatures and current rates. Another electrochemical method called aging-effect coupling models also can be used to analyze SEI film growth and other degradation mechanisms to provide accurate SOH estimations [21]. However, despite their high accuracy and detailed representation of internal mechanisms, electrochemical models often struggle to meet real-time requirements due to their limited adaptability to rapidly changing operational conditions.

To overcome those limitations, recent studies have introduced adaptive frameworks that incorporate real-time correction mechanisms into physics-based models. Wang et al. proposed an electrochemical–thermal coupling model with real-time coefficient correction that integrates multi-feature observations (voltage, current, temperature) into a recursive correction loop [22]. This model embeds temperature-sensitive correction terms directly into the state equations, improving the fidelity of parameter updates and enabling more accurate predictions at low temperatures and under nonstationary dynamics. Despite of their accuracy, this model is highly complex and computationally intensive, making them unsuitable for real-time BMS deployment [23].

To simplify physic-based model implementation, equivalent circuit models (ECMs) can be utilized [24]. These models represent the battery using a network of electrical components including resistors, capacitors, voltage sources. This equivalent circuit can be adapted to reflect aging behavior through parameter variation [25]. When combined with state estimation techniques such as the extended kalman filter (EKF) [45], unscented kalman filter (UKF) [46], or the particle filter (PF) [47], ECMs can provide real-time estimation of battery health and degradation. These methods can estimate internal battery states and age-dependent electrochemical model parameters using only current and voltage data collected over randomized discharge profiles, which is crucial for real-time monitoring. However, ECM accuracy may decrease under variable operating conditions, and parameter identification remains sensitive to noise, temperature, and load conditions [26].

Data-driven models have gained traction in battery modeling due to their ability to model complex nonlinear relationships without explicit physical equations [6]. These advantages can overcome the limitation of the physic-based method. These models learn from historical cycling data to predict capacity degradation [48]. Early studies used support vector machines (SVM) [27] and gaussian process regression (GPR) [28] for battery health and degradation prediction. SVM performs well with small datasets but lacks flexibility in modeling nonlinear trends. GPR, while offering uncertainty quantification, suffers from high computational cost and scalability issues.

Recent research has increasingly adopted deep learning techniques, artificial neural networks (ANN) [29] and recurrent neural networks (RNN), particularly long short-term memory (LSTM) networks [30] [31], have demonstrated strong performance in capturing temporal patterns of degradation. These models excel in long-term prediction with limited early cycles data. Deep learning technique also allows battery health diagnosis employing image-based method. Representing battery data as images allows the use of convolutional neural networks (CNN) for accurate degradation diagnosis, achieving lower root mean squared errors compared to other methods [32]. However, these methods require substantial data for training and suffer from interpretability challenges [34] [49]. In addition, deep learning methods for battery modeling are prone to overfitting and are sensitive to hyperparameter selection [35] [50]. Integrating transformers or encoder-decoder frameworks into deep learning approach can overcome these limitations. A research using NARX Transformer model presents an advanced framework by integrating inputs with a transformer-based encoder–decoder architecture to enhance long-term SOH and end-of-life (EoL) prediction across multiple battery cycles [33]. This approach mitigates overfitting and sensitivity to hyperparameter selection by explicitly incorporating domain-informed temporal dependencies and exogenous inputs, enabling more structured learning and improved generalization in battery degradation modeling. Nevertheless, the dependence on extensive multicycle datasets and computational complexity of this approach become significant challenges for real-time deployment and applicability in data-scarce environments [51].

More recently, ensemble learning algorithms, such as random forests (RF) [38] and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) [36] have shown high performance in solving regression problems including battery degradation. In battery degradation modeling, XGBoost is able to handle missing data and resistance to overfitting. It also can accurately capture battery behavior with limited number of dataset [52]. Another work has shown that XGBoost can effectively estimate the degradation pattern of lithium-ion batteries using early-cycle data with strong predictive performance [37]. This highlights the strength of XGBoost in capturing degradation patterns efficiently while the amount of data is limited. However, these ensemble methods often struggle with unseen usage patterns, sensor drift, and measurement noise that are common in real-world battery operations. Moreover, most models are trained offline and lack online correction capabilities, which limits their deployment in adaptive environments.

Techniques such as grey relational analysis (GRA) and principal component analysis (PCA) have been employed to remove redundant data and reduce dimensionality [48,53]. These methods improve model efficiency by minimizing computational overhead and focusing on the most relevant features. However, the preprocessing steps can add complexity to the modeling pipeline and may require significant domain expertise to implement effectively [54]. These shortcomings highlight the need for hybrid solutions that can balance the strengths of both physics-based and data-driven approaches [55].

Hybrid approaches aim to combine the strengths of physics-based models with data-driven techniques to enhance robustness and accuracy. These approaches have been proposed by several researcher. Ashidqi et al. proposed hybrid method using perceptron and thevenin equivalent circuit model [39]. Similarly, Fan et al. introduced a physics-informed integrated modeling method that combines electrochemical modeling with machine learning to improve degradation predictions [42]. These methods demonstrated enhanced accuracy, and computationally lightweight. However, the hybrid models developed based on electrochemical models require high computational cost due to its large number of parameters.

For short-term prediction and state estimation, ECMs were extensively used instead of electrochemical models due to its efficient computation [56]. There are various types of ECMs including simple linear model [57], the first order thevenin (R-C) model [58], the second order R-C model [59], and PNGV model [60]. ECMs are computationally efficient, require fewer parameters and suitable for real-time applications [56]. To implement hybrid approaches for dynamic model of battery degradation, physic-based model using ECM can be utilized [61] [40] [62]. The dynamic equation from ECM then can be combined with ML technique to create a grey-box model [41] [63] [64]. This model achieved improved accuracy for dynamic parameters estimation with efficient computation. However, despite the advancements achieved by these studies, several challenges remain unaddressed. The integration of data-driven methods with ECMs still faces difficulties in ensuring simplicity and adaptability without sacrificing accuracy [23].

The physics model such as ECM can be developed using state estimation methods including particle filtering (PF) and kalman filter (KF). KF then can be incorporated into data-driven architectures. For example, Meng et al. [43] proposed a hybrid GPR-KF framework, where a GPR model performs the initial capacity prediction, followed by refinement using a KF to adjust for real-time noise and state transitions. Similarly, another study used a back propagation neural network to estimate battery health and incorporated an Dual-EKF to improve prediction accuracy under dynamic load profiles and irregular charge cycles [65]. KF offer recursive correction capabilities, making them valuable in mitigating prediction drift due to environmental or usage variability [66]. These filters are particularly effective in tracking degradation over time while integrating new observations to update the system state [67].

Recent advances in state estimation techniques have significantly enhanced the accuracy, stability, and adaptability of KF-based frameworks for lithium-ion battery applications. Wang et al. [44] proposed an improved particle swarm optimization–adaptive square root cubature kalman filter (PSO-ASRCKF) for accurate state-of-charge (SOC) estimation. By jointly optimizing the process and measurement noise covariance matrices in real-time, this method enhances convergence speed and robustness against measurement noise. The integration of particle swarm optimization (PSO) enables dynamic adjustment of filter parameters by adaptively minimizing the trace of the a posteriori covariance matrix, thus ensuring greater filter stability under variable operating conditions. In a separate study, the same authors introduced a square-root untraced kalman filtering (SR-UKF) strategy for state-of-power (SOP) estimation, with full online identification of key battery parameters including capacity, internal resistance, and thermal coefficients [68]. The use of square-root factorization preserves the numerical stability of the filter by maintaining the positive semi-definiteness of the covariance matrix during recursive updates. Another study by Hou et al. [69] combined XGBoost with an adaptive cubature kalman filter (ACKF) to estimate SOC with improved nonlinear mapping, generalization ability, and real-time correction. In this framework, XGBoost models the complex nonlinear relationship between input features and SOC, while the ACKF performs online corrections to reduce prediction drift. The resulting system demonstrates robust performance in terms of accuracy, error convergence, and real-time tracking across laboratory and field conditions. These hybrid frameworks demonstrate the effectiveness of coupling physical modeling with adaptive recursive filters, enabling more reliable estimation under dynamic electrochemical and thermal conditions.

While the aforementioned methods provide strong solutions for SOC and SOP estimation, their direct application to battery degradation modeling remains limited due to fundamental differences in estimation objectives and temporal characteristics. SOC and SOP represent dynamic states that evolve over short timescales and can be directly influenced by instantaneous operating conditions, making them suitable for estimation using recursive filters with observable, real-time data. In contrast, battery degradation which is typically quantified through capacity decline or internal resistance growth is a slow and cumulative process that unfolds over extended cycles and is influenced by complex, nonlinear aging mechanisms such as SEI growth, lithium plating, and electrode degradation [70]. These degradation patterns are often masked by short-term operating noise, temperature fluctuations, and usage variability, which pose challenges for both observability and real-time estimation [71].

The proposed XGBoost–EKF framework presents a novel hybrid approach for lithium-ion battery degradation modeling by combining the nonlinear learning ability of XGBoost with the real-time correction capability of the EKF. Although both EKF and XGBoost have been individually applied in battery modeling, their use in degradation prediction remains limited. EKF is primarily employed for dynamic state estimation such as SOC or SOP, but has not been widely extended to long-term degradation processes due to its sensitivity to noise and modeling assumptions. Similarly, XGBoost has shown strong performance in learning degradation patterns from limited datasets, yet its predictions are purely offline and often lack adaptability under real-world operating variability. Furthermore, XGBoost is known to be sensitive to extreme initial data, which may lead to biased degradation predictions. In this work, we mitigate this issue by combining XGBoost with EKF's recursive correction mechanism, where internal resistance feedback is used to adaptively adjust predictions and reduce the influence of outlier effects. This motivates our hybrid design: by integrating XGBoost with EKF, we aim to combine the nonlinear pattern recognition capability of XGBoost with the robustness and physical interpretability of EKF. Unlike prior works focusing on state estimation or cycle-to-cycle prediction, this study applies the combination specifically to capacity degradation modeling, addressing the gap between short-term dynamic state estimation and long-term health prognosis. This integration enables accurate and interpretable SOH estimation by capturing long-term degradation patterns from historical data while adaptively correcting predictions in real time, thereby overcoming challenges such as data sensitivity, limited interpretability, and instability in recursive filters.

In summary, the novelty and specific advantages of this study can be outlined as follows:
•
A hybrid degradation modeling framework is introduced by integrating Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) with the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for lithium-ion battery capacity degradation prediction. This integration combines the nonlinear pattern learning capability of data-driven models with the adaptive correction and physical interpretability of recursive filters.
•
Unlike standalone XGBoost models that provide only offline predictions, the proposed framework incorporates EKF to enable real-time correction using internal resistance feedback. This mechanism enhances robustness against measurement noise and improves the interpretability of the degradation trajectory.
•
Compared to conventional physics-based recursive filters that often rely on simplified assumptions and are susceptible to instability, the proposed approach leverages XGBoost to capture complex nonlinear degradation behaviors from empirical data, thereby reducing dependence on rigid model structures.
•
This study represents one of the first applications of XGBoost–EKF integration specifically for lithium-ion battery degradation modeling and state-of-health (SOH) estimation, bridging the gap between short-term dynamic state estimation and long-term health prognosis.
•
The framework is trained and validated using a primary dataset obtained from controlled laboratory experiments, which ensures reliability, reproducibility, and applicability of the captured degradation patterns for real-world energy storage and electric vehicle systems.

In this paper, the structure is organized as follows: Section 2 describes the methodology for predicting battery degradation, Section 3 provides the results and discussion encompassing both experimental observations and model evaluation, and Section 4 concludes the study and outlines potential future work.

The prediction step framework of the proposed method is as shown in Fig. 1 . From this framework, it can be inferred that there are several steps that should be conducted in this research including experimental setup, dataset preparation, XGBoost modeling and KF modeling. Each steps are explained in the subsections below.

Various parameters can be utilized to assess capacity degradation during charge and discharge cycles. To identify these parameters, an experimental setup was developed using lithium-ferrous phosphate (LFP) cells with a nominal capacity of 1.8 Ah, manufactured by the Lithium Battery Research and Technology Centre at Sebelas Maret University in Surakarta, Indonesia. These battery cells are specifically designed for applications in electric vehicles and other energy storage systems [72]. Through this experiment, the actual degradation data along with several battery parameters can be collected and will serve as the basis for training and testing the model developed in the subsequent stage. The specifications of the LFP battery used in the experiment are presented in Table 2 .

The tools and materials used in this step including:
1.
LFP 18650 Battery produced by UNS Lithium Battery Research and Technology excellence center
2.
NEWARE Battery Tester unit
3.
NEWARE data acquisition software
These tools and materials are as shown in Fig. 2 .

The experiments were repeatedly conducted through 2000 continuous charge and discharge cycles using the constant current method, without any rest intervals. The cut-off voltage was set at 3.7 V for charging and 2.7 V for discharging. The cycles were conducted at a 2C-rate discharging and 1C-rate charging, where 1C represents the current required to fully charge or discharge the battery within one hour.

The aim of this experiment is to Measure various parameters through repeated cycling profiles to gather data that will assist in battery degradation modeling using the proposed method. The parameters acquired from this experiment including charging/discharging current, terminal voltage, cycle number and SOC or battery capacity. The SOC of the battery were measured by battery tester using coulomb counting method.

The method proposed in this research requires data with parameters including charging/discharging current, terminal voltage, cycle number and SOC or battery capacity. These parameters were obtained from experiment. However, there were several additional parameters required as the features for XGBoost training and KF initial parameters. These additional parameters should be derived from the primary parameters obtained from the experiment through a dataset preparation process.

From the voltage response to a current step, the settling time τ is measured when the voltage reaches approximately 63% of its final value, following the standard first-order system response of an RC circuit.

To estimate the capacity degradation of lithium-ion batteries, a machine learning approach based on XGBoost is employed. XGBoost is a scalable and efficient implementation of gradient-boosted decision trees, known for its superior performance in tabular data regression tasks and its robustness against multicollinearity, outliers, and nonlinear relationships. The method is particularly effective in capturing complex statistical patterns that are not easily modeled by physics-based equations.

The model input consists of statistical features extracted from current and voltage signals over fixed-length operation cycles. These features include:
•
Minimum, maximum, and mean values of the voltage and current,
•

While XGBoost is highly effective at mapping statistical patterns in battery signals to capacity degradation, it is inherently data-driven and lacks physical interpretability. To address this limitation, a correction mechanism is introduced using internal resistance (

The coefficient α is empirically determined to scale this physical signal appropriately for correcting the model output. Although not derived from a specific electrochemical equation, the correction is grounded in observed physical trends and provides a practical way to embed real-time degradation information into the data-driven prediction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for degradation tracking
ce:section.ce:para:
Since the internal resistance is a non-linear function of the battery degradation, an EKF is used. The EKF linearizes the model using a first-order Taylor expansion and updates the Jacobian matrices dynamically during estimation. The nonlinear measurement function derived from the Thevenin model is:

This derivation confirms how each element of the Jacobian corresponds to physical terms in the measurement model. This approach enables accurate tracking of battery health parameters and provides robust predictions under operational uncertainties.

This subsection presents the overall framework of the proposed capacity degradation prediction method, which integrates XGBoost and KF-based correction to enhance prediction accuracy and reliability. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the framework consists of two main components: a data-driven degradation prediction model and a correction mechanism based on internal resistance estimation. The prediction is initially performed using an XGBoost regressor trained on statistical features extracted from battery operational data, while the correction is applied using an EKF to align predictions with the battery physical behavior. The complete procedure is formalized in Algorithm 1 , which summarizes the steps from feature extraction and model training to internal resistance-based correction for the final estimation of capacity loss.

Fig. 4 presents the evolution of the terminal voltage
V
t
of the lithium-ion battery at selected cycle numbers (Cycle 50, 100, 200, and 300). These curves are obtained under controlled charge-discharge cycles using the constant current method. As cycling progresses, the voltage profiles during discharge exhibit significant changes: the voltage drop becomes steeper, and the end-of-discharge voltage is reached more quickly. These patterns indicate an increase in internal resistance and degradation of electrochemical performance—typical aging signatures in lithium-ion cells. The earlier voltage collapse observed at later cycles suggests the presence of degradation mechanisms such as loss of active lithium, SEI growth, and electrode deterioration, which collectively reduce the battery's ability to maintain terminal voltage under load.

Fig. 5 shows the cumulative capacity loss across 300 cycles. Initially, the capacity degradation follows an approximately linear trajectory up to Cycle 200. Afterward, the degradation rate accelerates, resulting in a total capacity loss of more than 6 Ah by the final cycle. This transition from linear to nonlinear degradation is consistent with findings in the literature, often attributed to the intensification of side reactions such as SEI layer thickening, lithium plating, and structural breakdown of the electrodes. These reactions exacerbate internal resistance and reduce effective lithium-ion transport, leading to rapid decline in performance.

The trends in Figs. 4 and 5 are closely interrelated. As internal resistance increases with cycling, a greater overpotential is needed to sustain the same current. This results in faster voltage drops during discharge and consequently lowers usable capacity, even when the battery is fully charged. The evolving voltage profile thus reflects the battery's internal aging state and serves as a key observable for non-invasive SOH estimation.

These experimental findings reinforce the need for intelligent degradation modeling frameworks that can extract, learn, and predict such complex and nonlinear degradation behavior. Traditional physics-based models may not capture the full variability in operational data, while purely data-driven models often lack physical consistency. Therefore, a hybrid approach—such as the one proposed in this work—offers a promising solution. By extracting statistical features (e.g., minimum, maximum, mean, skewness, and kurtosis) from voltage and current signals, and coupling machine learning predictions with internal resistance feedback using an EKF, the proposed framework ensures both high prediction accuracy and physical interpretability. This justifies the realization of a data-driven yet physically informed model as a practical and robust method for real-time battery health monitoring.

The XGBoost model employed in this study utilizes a set of statistical features derived from voltage and current signals, as defined in Equation (1). These features, extracted from each battery cycle, include standard metrics (maximum, minimum, mean) as well as higher-order moments (skewness and kurtosis). As outlined in Subsection 2.2, this feature construction aims to represent the operational behavior of the battery under cycling conditions.

To ensure that the model is both efficient and effective, a correlation analysis is conducted to assess the degree of linear dependency between features. This step is crucial for identifying potential redundancy in the input data and ensuring that each feature contributes unique information to the model. Fig. 6 shows the Pearson correlation heatmap for all extracted features used in capacity loss estimation. Pearson correlation is chosen for its simplicity and interpretability in evaluating pairwise linear relationships, with values ranging from −1 (perfect negative correlation) to 1 (perfect positive correlation), and 0 indicating no correlation.

From the heatmap, it is evident that several voltage-based features—such as max ⁡ ( V ) , min ⁡ ( V ) , and μ ( V ) —are highly correlated (correlation coefficients exceeding 0.80). A similar trend is observed for current-based features like max ⁡ ( I ) , min ⁡ ( I ) , and μ ( I ) . This indicates that some of these features may offer overlapping information, potentially introducing redundancy into the model. In contrast, voltage and current features tend to exhibit moderate to strong negative correlations (approximately −0.50 to −0.60), which is consistent with the opposing behavior of voltage and current during charge and discharge cycles.

More importantly, the higher-order features—such as skewness ( V ) , kurtosis ( V ) , skewness ( I ) , and kurtosis ( I ) —show weak or no correlation with the basic statistical metrics (e.g., mean or max values). This implies that these features contribute independent information regarding the shape and distribution of the signals, which is particularly valuable for capturing the nonlinear and irregular patterns induced by battery ageing. For instance, changes in skewness may indicate asymmetric charging/discharging behaviors, while variations in kurtosis may reflect the presence of spikes or heavy tails in the signal distributions—phenomena often linked to degradation events like SEI growth or capacity fading.

Based on these insights, the inclusion of skewness and kurtosis in the model is well justified, as they enhance the ability of XGBoost to learn degradation-related non-linearities. While feature selection could be considered to eliminate redundancies among highly correlated features (e.g., choosing one representative among max ⁡ ( V ) , min ⁡ ( V ) , and μ ( V ) ), all features are retained in this study to allow XGBoost inherent regularization and tree-based splitting mechanism to perform effective internal feature selection during training.

In conclusion, the correlation analysis validates the feature set by confirming that while some features are redundant, others—particularly the higher-order statistical descriptors—offer unique and essential information. This balance of redundancy and uniqueness supports the realization of an interpretable and physically meaningful data-driven model. It enables the XGBoost framework to capture complex degradation dynamics, thereby strengthening the innovation of the proposed hybrid approach that integrates machine learning with physics-informed correction via EKF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybrid capacity loss prediction and evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the proposed hybrid degradation estimation framework, the dataset consisting of 2000 battery charge-discharge cycles was divided into three subsets using stratified cycle ordering: 70% of the data (first 1400 cycles) was used for training, 15% (next 300 cycles) for validation, and the remaining 15% (last 300 cycles) for testing. This sequential division reflects a realistic operational scenario, where a model is trained on early-to-mid life data and then deployed to predict degradation during later stages. A 5-fold cross-validation was also conducted on the training data to fine-tune the hyperparameters of the XGBoost model and avoid overfitting.

Model performance is evaluated using three standard metrics: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), and the coefficient of determination (
R
2
). These metrics quantify both the accuracy of capacity loss predictions and the degree to which the model captures underlying degradation trends. The XGBoost model was first trained using extracted statistical features, then enhanced through post-processing with a Kalman filter using real-time internal resistance estimates to refine the prediction.

In this framework, the XGBoost algorithm is used to predict long-term capacity loss (
Q
loss
) based on degradation features, while the Kalman filter is employed to estimate the internal resistance online using the first-order Thevenin equivalent circuit model. The outputs of internal resistance (
R
0
) estimation are used as the correction for XGBoost capacity loss prediction. Therefore, the consistency and reliability of the degradation prediction can be enhanced.

The integration of KF-based internal resistance estimation as a correction mechanism into the XGBoost framework provides a key innovation in this study. As described in Section 2.4 and detailed in Algorithm 1, the EKF is used to estimate the internal resistance
R
0
of the battery over time, based on the first-order Thevenin equivalent circuit model. This real-time resistance estimate captures the dynamic and cumulative effects of electrochemical ageing.

In the hybrid prediction architecture, the output of the XGBoost model—which estimates capacity loss (
Q
loss
) from statistical voltage and current features—is corrected using the internal resistance estimate from the EKF stage. The correction is applied cycle-by-cycle, such that the final prediction integrates both the data-driven degradation pattern and a physically grounded ageing indicator. This setup ensures that predictions remain aligned with the real internal state of the battery, even under varying operating or loading conditions.

In the experimental implementation, the correction term is realized using the
R
0
values obtained from the EKF process. These values are fused with the XGBoost output following the correction algorithm, thereby refining the predicted degradation trajectory and ensuring physical consistency across cycles. Fig. 7 shows the scatter plot of
R
0
as a function of
Q
loss
. The plot exhibits a clear linear relationship, where the internal resistance increases proportionally with the loss in battery capacity. This trend aligns with established electrochemical degradation mechanisms in lithium-ion batteries, including SEI growth, loss of active material, and increased electronic transport resistance. These mechanisms contribute to both rising internal resistance and decreasing usable capacity over cycles.

The experimental verification of internal resistance behavior is presented in Fig. 7, which demonstrates a strong linear correlation between the estimated
R
0
and the measured capacity loss. This result confirms that the resistance values obtained from the EKF estimation are consistent with experimentally observed ageing mechanisms. The proportional increase of
R
0
with
Q
loss
validates the use of internal resistance as a reliable correction indicator for degradation prediction, thereby strengthening the physical grounding of the proposed hybrid framework.

The results indicate that the proposed hybrid framework not only improves prediction accuracy but also enhances robustness in modeling degradation behavior. Fig. 8 presents a comparative analysis between two models: (i) the baseline XGBoost model and (ii) the hybrid XGBoost + EKF model. The experimental capacity degradation (blue line) is used as the ground truth, while the orange dashed and red solid lines represent the XGBoost-only and EKF-corrected predictions, respectively.

The Kalman-corrected curve (red) follows the true degradation trajectory more closely and exhibits significantly fewer fluctuations, particularly in the mid-to-late cycle range (Cycles 1000–2000). This suppression of high-frequency noise and elimination of non-physical oscillations yield a smoother and more monotonic degradation trend, which is consistent with the gradual and irreversible nature of lithium-ion battery ageing. In contrast, the XGBoost-only model shows local deviations and spurious variance due to sensitivity to noise and overfitting to short-term patterns.

Robustness in this context refers to the ability of the model to maintain stable and physically consistent predictions under varying conditions, rather than producing erratic fluctuations when exposed to noisy or complex data. The EKF correction achieves this by introducing temporal consistency and anchoring predictions to electrochemical reality through the internal resistance parameter
R
0
, a well-established degradation indicator. This correction mechanism reduces the influence of extreme data points, mitigates overfitting effects, and ensures predictions remain both accurate and physically plausible. Consequently, the hybrid XGBoost–EKF framework provides not only improved accuracy but also enhanced robustness and reliability, which are critical for real-world deployment in energy storage and electric vehicle applications.

From a quantitative point of view, the performance of the proposed method is measured using three metrics: root mean square error (RMSE), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and coefficient of determination (
R
2
). The result of these metrics is shown in Table 4 . The proposed method achieves significantly lower RMSE and MAPE values, indicating higher accuracy in predicting battery capacity degradation. It also provides tighter error bounds and more consistent predictions across the dataset. Furthermore, the
R
2
of 0.98 highlights the strong ability of the model to capture the underlying data trends, as compared to the 0.93 achieved by the standard XGBoost. This substantial improvement can be attributed to the integration of the EKF within the XGBoost framework, which enhances its capability to track dynamic behavior and filter noise—thereby improving generalization and robustness in real-world battery degradation estimation tasks.

As illustrated in Fig. 9 , the proposed XGBoost–EKF model demonstrates its strong capability to learn and generalize from degradation data under different current rates (0.5C, 1C, and 2C). Each current rate exhibits distinct degradation characteristics where higher C-rates accelerate capacity loss due to increased internal resistance and electrochemical stress yet the model is able to accurately follow each trend. The EKF-based correction allows the model to dynamically adapt to the internal resistance variation induced by different loading conditions, leading to smooth and realistic prediction curves. This indicates that the proposed hybrid method not only captures the nonlinear degradation relationship but also effectively handles the stochastic variation in experimental measurements.

Moreover, the consistent alignment between the predicted and measured curves across all current profiles highlights the robustness and adaptability of the proposed approach. Unlike conventional data-driven models that often overfit to specific operating regimes, the XGBoost–EKF framework preserves high accuracy even when trained on heterogeneous datasets with varying stress levels. This adaptability confirms the model's ability to learn the underlying degradation dynamics rather than merely memorizing patterns, making it well-suited for real-world scenarios where batteries experience diverse loading profiles. In addition, the smoothness of the prediction lines suggests that the EKF efficiently filters noise without oversmoothing the degradation trend, ensuring both precision and physical plausibility in the estimated capacity trajectories.

This study proposes a hybrid framework that integrates XGBoost with the EKF to enhance the accuracy and robustness of battery capacity estimation. To evaluate its performance, a comparative analysis was conducted against widely adopted machine learning techniques, including random forest (RF), nonlinear auto-regressive with exogenous inputs (NARXs), long short-term memory (LSTM), gaussian process regression with kalman filter (GPR-KF), and optimized XGBoost. Each method was assessed in terms of both prediction accuracy and computational efficiency. The results are summarized in Table 5 and Figs. 10 and 11
.

From an accuracy perspective, the proposed XGBoost+EKF model achieves the lowest RMSE (0.041) and MAPE (3.47%), demonstrating a significant improvement over both traditional ensemble and deep learning models. As shown in Table 5 and Fig. 10, NARXs and LSTM models show relatively competitive MAPE values (3.71% and 3.78%, respectively), yet their higher RMSE scores (0.073 and 0.091) indicate less consistent performance across the cycle range. This discrepancy underscores the benefit of the EKF in refining the raw XGBoost output: the filter effectively captures temporal dependencies and corrects accumulated errors by incorporating real-time resistance feedback. In contrast, models that rely solely on historical sequence learning may underperform in noisy or unstructured environments.

Beyond predictive accuracy, computational performance is a key requirement for real-time applications in embedded battery management systems. As shown in Fig. 11, the proposed method achieves the lowest inference time (2.1 ms), which is approximately 3–4 times faster than LSTM (8.3 ms) and NARXs (7.2 ms). Additionally, it maintains a low memory footprint (34 MB) and a compact model size of 0.38 million parameters, making it highly suitable for deployment in microcontroller-based platforms or edge AI hardware. These computational gains are critical for minimizing latency and power consumption in resource-constrained systems.

The strength of the proposed hybrid model lies in its architectural synergy, where the XGBoost serves as a powerful learner for extracting nonlinear degradation features from voltage and current signals, while the EKF provides dynamic correction based on physical battery behavior. This complementary interaction allows the model to maintain both high prediction fidelity and robustness to outliers or unmodeled variations. Importantly, the EKF enhancement acts not as a replacement, but as a real-time correction layer, enhancing the generalization of the learned mapping under varying operating conditions.

Furthermore, the superiority of the proposed method is evident when compared to other ensemble approaches such as Optimized XGBoost and RF. Although these models offer faster inference than deep networks, they lack the temporal adaptability needed to track progressive degradation. For instance, Optimized XGBoost records the highest RMSE (0.120), suggesting that static feature optimization alone is insufficient to address time-varying capacity loss. The integration of EKF in our approach bridges this gap by incorporating recursive state estimation, which reduces prediction drift and cumulative error over long-term use.

In summary, the proposed XGBoost + EKF framework outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives in both prediction accuracy and computational efficiency. Its lightweight structure, fast inference, and robustness to degradation dynamics make it highly promising for embedded applications. The method provides a well-balanced trade-off between model complexity, real-time applicability, and accuracy, making it suitable for next-generation battery management systems in electric vehicles, renewable energy storage, and portable electronics.

This study presents a novel hybrid framework that combines XGBoost with an EKF to improve the estimation of lithium-ion battery capacity degradation. The core innovation lies in using the EKF to estimate internal resistance in real time, which is then used to guide and correct the output of the XGBoost model. This approach bridges the gap between data-driven learning and physics-based interpretation. Experimental results over 2000 cycles of constant-current charge–discharge data demonstrate strong performance, with the proposed model achieving an RMSE of 0.041, a MAPE of 3.47%, and a high coefficient of determination (
R
2
= 0.98 ), outperforming several state-of-the-art methods including LSTM, NARXs, and optimized XGBoost.

Beyond predictive accuracy, the hybrid model also improves the physical consistency of degradation predictions by reducing high-frequency fluctuations and maintaining a monotonic aging trend. The EKF correction enhances temporal stability, which is essential for reliable long-term monitoring. Furthermore, the framework shows strong potential for real-time deployment, requiring only 2.1 milliseconds of inference time, with low memory usage and a compact model size. These characteristics make the method suitable for embedded applications such as electric vehicle battery management systems and grid-scale storage units where computational efficiency and prediction reliability are both critical.

However, the current experimental validation was conducted under idealized laboratory conditions—constant current cycling at a fixed ambient temperature of 20
∘
C—which may not reflect real-world operating environments. In practice, electric vehicle batteries are subject to highly dynamic and variable conditions, including fluctuating loads, temperatures, and usage patterns. Future work will involve extending this framework to more diverse datasets that incorporate real driving profiles, multi-temperature scenarios, and different battery chemistries to verify the robustness and the generalizability of the model in practical applications.
